figures.gloss: Japanese thunder-god, ringed by the drums he beats to make the storm.
figures.facet: Thunder rendered as literal percussion: the red demon ringed by taiko drums makes the storm's sound the god's own instrument, not a voice or a hurled weapon.
figures.provenance: Kojiki (712) and Nihon Shoki (720), the ikazuchi (thunder deities) born on Izanami's decaying body in Yomi; codified pictorially in Tawaraya Sotatsu's Fujin Raijin-zu screens (17th century).

figures.gloss: Tempestuous storm-god, exiled brother of the sun, who slays the eight-headed serpent Yamata-no-Orochi.
figures.facet: Banishment into heroism: the same violent unruliness that gets him expelled from heaven for ritual pollution is what makes him effective against the chaos-serpent on earth — exile converts a liability into a monster-slayer.
figures.provenance: Kojiki (712) and Nihon Shoki (720), the Izumo cycle; the Yamata-no-Orochi episode and the drawing of Kusanagi from the serpent's tail.

figures.gloss: The sun goddess from whom Japan's emperors descend; when she hides in her cave, all the world goes dark.
figures.facet: The sun as she — and as ancestral sovereign — overturning the usual male-solar pattern in a single stroke.

figures.gloss: The primordial male kami who, with his sister-consort Izanami, stirs the primal brine with the jeweled spear Ame-no-nuboko to form the first island, then fathers the Japanese archipelago and its kami with her.
figures.facet: Fatherhood by hygiene rather than decree: Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi, and Susanoo are not born of a marital act at all but precipitate from the act of washing pollution off his own body in a river — creation completed as an afterthought of purification.
figures.provenance: Kojiki (712); Nihon Shoki (720), the Izanagi-Izanami cosmogony.

figures.gloss: Izanagi's sister-consort and co-creator, who dies giving birth to the fire kami Kagutsuchi and descends to Yomi, the land of the dead; when Izanagi breaks his vow and sees her decomposing body there, she pursues him in rage and becomes Yomi's sovereign.
figures.facet: Her rotting body, glimpsed against her will, founds the entire purity/pollution (kegare) axis that harae ritually manages ever after; the vow she and Izanagi exchange at Yomi's blocked entrance — she killing a thousand a day, he causing fifteen hundred births — is the myth's literal arithmetic for why death and birth never balance in humanity's favor.

figures.gloss: The moon kami, born from Izanagi's right eye during his purification, completing the triad of noble children with Amaterasu and Susanoo.
figures.facet: The most silent of the three siblings in surviving myth, defined almost entirely by rupture: in the Nihon Shoki he kills the food goddess Uke Mochi in disgust at how she produces her feast, and Amaterasu, appalled, vows never to look on him again — the etiological moment that permanently separates day from night, sun from moon.
figures.provenance: Nihon Shoki (720), the Tsukuyomi/Uke Mochi episode.

figures.gloss: Kami of rice, agriculture, and prosperity, worshipped at more enshrined sites than any other kami in Japan, centered on Fushimi Inari Taisha's mountain of vermilion torii tunnels; served by fox (kitsune) messengers, and depicted variously as male, female, or androgynous depending on region and era.
figures.facet: A single cult so numerous it is barely singular: Inari worship fragments into thousands of independently vowed shrines and lineages (including Buddhist Dakiniten identifications), making the kami less one deity than a franchise of local prosperity-pacts bearing one name.
figures.provenance: Karen Smyers, The Fox and the Jewel (1999); Fushimi Inari Taisha's own shrine history.

figures.gloss: Kami of archery and war, identified with the legendary Emperor Ojin and enshrined nationally from Usa Hachiman-gu; patron of the Minamoto clan and, by extension, the samurai class.
figures.facet: The paradigm case of shinbutsu-shugo made into a title: for centuries Hachiman was venerated as 'Hachiman Daibosatsu' (Great Bodhisattva Hachiman), a kami formally granted Buddhist rank — the fusion doctrine other traditions state abstractly, here conferred as an actual honorific.
figures.provenance: Usa Hachiman-gu shrine history; Allan Grapard's scholarship on kami-buddha combinatory cults.

figures.gloss: Lord of the Izumo cycle: master-builder and pacifier of the visible land (kuni-zukuri), who cedes political rule to Amaterasu's descendants (kuni-yuzuri) in exchange for the construction of Izumo Taisha, the great shrine that rivals Ise; also kami of marriage, medicine, and magic.
figures.facet: The land-cession is not defeat but a bargain: Okuninushi trades visible sovereignty for permanent lordship of the unseen world (kakuriyo) and the largest shrine in the land — the myth's way of admitting that Izumo's real power outlived its political submission to Yamato.
figures.provenance: Kojiki (712), the Izumo cycle; Izumo Taisha's shrine tradition.

figures.gloss: The dawn-dancer whose comic, semi-nude performance atop an overturned tub — stamping until the assembled kami roar with laughter — lures the hidden Amaterasu out of the rock cave and restores light to the world; ancestress of the Sarume clan and, by tradition, of court kagura.
figures.facet: The holy fool solves what force cannot: eight hundred myriad kami besiege the cave with offerings and mirrors, but it is bawdy comedy, not power or piety, that actually breaks the sun goddess's resolve.
figures.provenance: Kojiki (712); Nihon Shoki (720), the Ama-no-Iwato cave episode.

figures.gloss: Amaterasu's grandson, sent down from heaven to Mount Takachiho bearing the mirror, jewel, and sword — the three imperial regalia — to found the earthly dynasty; great-grandfather, by tradition, of the first emperor, Jimmu.
figures.facet: The tenson korin descent converts a myth of solar ancestry into a claim of terrestrial rule: everything the roster's Amaterasu gloss asserts about imperial descent runs, structurally, through this one unnamed hinge — a god who never rules, only founds.
figures.provenance: Kojiki (712); Nihon Shoki (720), the tenson korin narrative.

figures.gloss: The eight-headed, eight-tailed serpent of Izumo, drunk on sake and slain by the exiled Susanoo, from whose severed tail he draws the sword Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, one of the three imperial regalia.
figures.facet: A monster whose death is a metallurgical discovery as much as a heroic one: the myth's real payload is not the killing but the sword found inside the corpse, folding a chaoskampf into the origin story of a state treasure.
figures.provenance: Kojiki (712); Nihon Shoki (720), the Izumo cycle.

figures.gloss: The tradition's own category of the sacred: not a fixed pantheon but anything — deity, ancestor, mountain, storm, unusual stone — that inspires awe or reverence and is treated accordingly. Non-dogmatic and radically plural, with no single creed defining what a kami is.
figures.facet: The category refuses systematic theology by design: Motoori Norinaga's own 18th-century definition ('anything whatsoever which was outside the ordinary, which possessed superior power, or which was awe-inspiring') describes a feeling and a relation, not an ontology — making Shinto's central term a practice of attention rather than a doctrine of being.

figures.gloss: Rites of purification clearing kegare (pollution/impurity) rather than sin: the waved paper wand (haraigushi), water lustration (misogi), and the twice-yearly Great Purification (Oharae) performed nationally at midsummer and year's end.
figures.facet: No moral confession is required: harae treats impurity as a contaminant to be physically cleared rather than a wrong to be forgiven, tracing directly back to the river where Izanagi washed off Yomi's death-pollution and, in doing so, generated three of Shinto's greatest kami.
figures.provenance: Kojiki (712); ongoing shrine practice, e.g. the Nagoshi-no-Oharae and Oshogatsu purification rites.

figures.gloss: The 'Record of Ancient Matters' (712 CE), compiled for the court by O no Yasumaro from the recitations of Hieda no Are at Empress Genmei's commission; the oldest surviving written source for the kami cosmogony, the age of the gods, and the imperial genealogy from Amaterasu down.
figures.facet: A text commissioned to fix a genealogy: the mythology exists in writing at all because a court needed a settled, official account of how the sun goddess's line came to rule — the world's oldest Shinto scripture is, at root, a dynastic land-title.
figures.provenance: Kojiki, preface and text (712 CE), O no Yasumaro.

edges.note: Storm-hero slays the many-headed/coiling serpent to free the waters / win the realm.

Two FEMININE suns — Yhi and Amaterasu — both life-giving sun-women whose light is the world's first awakening, jointly upholding that the solar-as-mother is no anomaly but a recurring choice unrelated cultures make against the Indo-European sun-king.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Supreme solar deities from whom the imperial family claims direct, literal physical descent.
The hinge of which luminary rules: South Arabia raises the MOON to national high-god while Shinto raises the SUN to imperial ancestor — the same supreme-luminary office filled by opposite lights.
Two rare solar GODDESSES against the world's usual male sun: al-Lat as the sun-bright celestial mother and Amaterasu as the heavenly radiance — the feminine sun appearing independently in two shards.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both utilize the mirror as the ultimate aniconic representation of the divine, reflecting the sacred truth back onto the observer rather than relying on carved idols.
Two of the rare feminine solar deities — Saulė and Amaterasu — independent witnesses that the sun need not be male.
The goddess who crosses the sky as fire — solar/luminous sovereignty held by a female, against the more common male sun; a reach across utterly unrelated traditions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A stark inversion of the solar archetype; Amaterasu is the radiant ancestor of order, whereas the Chumash Sun is a malevolent, consuming gambler.
The sun honoured at the turning of the year for the renewal of life and grain — Poṅkal's solstice overflow and the Shinto solar rites, the harvest-sun celebrated independently.
Both luminary-deities head the bright order, but Fon assigns the calm sovereign light to the MOON and a female, where Shinto crowns the female SUN — the gendering of the luminaries reversed.
Ram-voiced thunder hurled from heaven; the lightning-striker as a recurring sky-figure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme solar deities whose temporary disappearance or capture plunges the world into a state of cosmic terror.
Two FEMALE suns sovereign over their pantheons — the Sun-Goddess of Arinna and Amaterasu both invert the usual male solar eye; Anatolia's sun is queen and patron of kingship.

A female Sun fleeing into or out of the dark over a sibling's offence — Malina chased by her Moon-brother, Amaterasu withdrawing from Susanoo's violence; the sun gendered female and driven by family rupture.

East-Asian sacred-descent kingship: as Amaterasu's heavenly grandchild founds the imperial line from the descent to the peak, so Hwanung's son Dangun founds the first Korean kingdom from the descent to the mountain — a strong structural rhyme of heaven-descended legitimacy, no descent or borrowing proven.

Both myths turn on a god's grandchild/son descending from heaven to a sacred mountain to found a people — the shared 'tenson korin'-type descent of East-Asian foundation myth, parallel structure without demonstrated contact.

Sun as chief of the holy powers and centre of the people's highest rite — Wí of the Sun Dance and Amaterasu of the imperial cult; the solar disk as the focus of a nation's worship in two unconnected worlds.

A loose sun-and-moon-separation echo: Haŋwí's parting from Wí sets day apart from night, faintly rhyming with the sun-hidden-and-restored dramas of solar myth; flagged, no shared structure beyond the day/night divide.
A solar-linked renewing goddess at the centre of her people's ceremony and identity — a faint parallel of the life-giving female power, with the seam tied to no shared descent.
Both turn on the sun's confinement and release — Amaterasu lured from the cave, the sun loosed from the box; the lit world restored by drawing light out of enclosure.
Thunder personified as a sky-being whose body makes the storm; both render thunder as embodied rather than instrumental.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme female divinities of heat and light who sit at the absolute apex of their pantheons, politically outranking male sky and storm gods.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions visually and conceptually identify the political sovereign directly with the unbroken light, power, and descent of the Sun.

Storm-deities attended by a herald of thunder — Guabancex preceded by Guataubá who marshals the winds and thunder, as the Japanese thunder-god rides the tempest — the storm given a thunderous outrider, separately conceived.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Tenrikyo initially adopted the vocabulary of Shinto kami, including the sun goddess, to explain its cosmology before revealing its monotheistic nature.
Both personify the tempest, but Raijin is a drum-beating thunder-spirit and Inkanyamba a serpent of the falls — same weather, very different body. Flagged.

SHINTO SEAM: under honji-suijaku the sun-goddess herself was identified as the trace of the cosmic buddha Dainichi (Mahavairocana) — Ryobu Shinto mapped Ise's two shrines onto the two mandalas — the paradigm case of the fusion Shugendo inhabits.

SHINTO SEAM (kin, not subset): two Japonic solar-and-sovereignty cults centered on sacred women — the kikoe-ogimi sanctifying the Ryukyuan king (himself styled tedako, 'child of the sun') as the sun-goddess and her Ise priestesses sanctify the Yamato line; kin developments from a shared archipelago substrate, neither derived from the other.

SHINTO SEAM: both official mythologies open with descent from heaven that makes islands and dynasties in one motion — Amamikyu lowered to build the Ryukyus as the Japanese cycle stirs its islands and sends Amaterasu's grandchild down to rule; parallel Japonic statecraft-cosmogony, not borrowing.

Oomoto exalts the banished primal kami (identified with Kunitokotachi of the Shinto chronicles) above the Amaterasu-centered imperial orthodoxy — a documented doctrinal inversion of State Shinto that the state prosecuted as lèse-majesté in 1921 and 1935.

Konkokyo grew inside the Shinto world and was sanctioned in 1900 as one of the thirteen Kyōha Shintō sects — its universal parent kami emerged from the folk-Shinto Konjin cult while its liturgy and priesthood remained Shinto in form.
dragon-slayers who claim a treasure from the serpent they kill — Sigurðr the cursed gold and ring Andvaranaut from Fáfnir, Susanoo the sword Kusanagi from the tail of Yamata-no-Orochi.
Intra-tradition: the primordial brother-sister creator couple who stir the sea and birth the islands and the kami together.
Intra-tradition: purified life (Izanagi, washed clean of Yomi) against ruling death (Izanami, sovereign of Yomi) — the pair's Yomi confrontation is the myth's stated origin of the death/birth ratio.
Intra-tradition: Amaterasu is born from Izanagi's left eye during his post-Yomi purification.
Intra-tradition: Tsukuyomi is born from Izanagi's right eye during the same purification.
Intra-tradition: Susanoo is born from Izanagi's nose during the same purification, completing the three noble children.
Intra-tradition: siblings permanently estranged after Tsukuyomi kills the food goddess Uke Mochi — the myth's origin of the sun and moon never sharing the sky.
Intra-tradition: storm-god slays the eight-headed serpent and draws the imperial sword Kusanagi from its tail — the roster's own cited chaoskampf, now with its adversary named.
Intra-tradition: Ame-no-Uzume's cave-mouth dance is the mechanism that lures Amaterasu out of hiding, restoring light to the world.
Intra-tradition: grandmother and grandson — Amaterasu sends Ninigi to earth with the three regalia to found the dynasty.
Intra-tradition: Okuninushi's land-cession (kuni-yuzuri) to Amaterasu's line is the precondition the myth cycle requires before Ninigi's heavenly descent can claim the earth.
Intra-tradition: Izanagi's river purification after fleeing Yomi is the myth-narrated origin of every misogi and harae rite performed since.
Intra-tradition: the 712 text is the source that fixes the imperial genealogy running down from Amaterasu, commissioned to settle that very claim.

SHINTO/SHUGENDO SEAM: Hachiman's centuries-long veneration as 'Great Bodhisattva Hachiman' is a paradigm instance, alongside Zao Gongen and Amaterasu-as-Dainichi, of the honji-suijaku fusion Shugendo institutionalizes.
Independent East/Southeast Asian rice deities anchoring national agricultural prosperity cults — Inari's fox-attended shrines and Mae Phosop's rice-goddess veneration, unrelated in origin.

Both name a broad, non-dogmatic category of the sacred-and-awesome rather than a closed pantheon — though kami tends toward discrete entities (gods, ancestors, places) where mana is explicitly conceived as an impersonal, transferable force; a structural rhyme, not identity.

dossier.essence: The indigenous religion of Japan, the 'way of the kami' — a diffuse, decentralized web of deities and spirits (kami) immanent in nature, ancestors, and place, with no founder, no creed, and no single scripture. Practiced by a large share of the Japanese population, usually alongside Buddhism, it centers on ritual purity and communal well-being rather than doctrine, and is organized around tens of thousands of shrines (jinja).
dossier.cosmos: The world is filled with innumerable kami — conventionally 'eight million' — who dwell in mountains, rivers, trees, stones, and remarkable persons. The classical myths of the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki tell of the generative pair Izanagi and Izanami, the birth of the islands, the sun-goddess Amaterasu from whom the imperial line claims descent, and her unruly storm-brother Susanoo. There is no fall and no final judgment; death brings pollution, and the dead may in time become ancestral spirits or kami, so the governing axis is purity against defilement (kegare) rather than sin against righteousness.
dossier.practice: Worship centers on the shrine and the seasonal festival (matsuri): purification (harae, misogi), offerings, and formal prayer (norito) to secure the kami's blessing, fertility, and protection. Priests tend the enshrined kami, households keep kamidana altars, and rites of passage — Shichi-Go-San, weddings, the new-year hatsumode — mark the calendar. Emphasis falls on ritual correctness, gratitude, and the cleansing of impurity rather than on ethical creed or belief.
dossier.canon: No revealed scripture; the principal early sources are the eighth-century imperial chronicles Kojiki (712) and Nihon Shoki (720), together with the Engishiki ritual codes and the Man'yōshū.
dossier.reflects: The holiness of place and purity — the sacred met as immanent presence in the given world and maintained by cleansing, not as rescue from it.
sources: Thomas P. Kasulis, 'Shinto: The Way Home' (2004)
